heroOverview: Penicillosis — caused by the thermal dimorphic fungus Talaromyces marneffei (formerly Penicillium marneffei) — is the third most common AIDS-defining opportunistic infection in Southeast Asia and southern China, after tuberculosis and cryptococcosis. Unlike most fungal pathogens, T. marneffei switches from a mould at ambient temperature to a dividing yeast-like form at body temperature, allowing it to replicate inside macrophages and disseminate through the reticuloendothelial system. The result is a systemic illness with skin lesions, fever, anaemia, weight loss, and hepatosplenomegaly that is clinically similar to disseminated tuberculosis or histoplasmosis. Left untreated in a person with HIV, disseminated penicillosis is fatal within weeks. With prompt antifungal therapy the great majority of patients recover, though relapse is common unless antiretroviral therapy succeeds in restoring CD4 immunity.

keyStats.prevalence: Accounts for approximately 15-25% of AIDS-defining illnesses in northern Thailand, Vietnam, and southern China; an estimated 8,000-17,000 new cases annually in Southeast Asia
keyStats.demographics: More than 90% of cases occur in HIV-positive individuals with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL; rare cases in solid-organ transplant recipients and patients on immunosuppressants
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases in adults aged 20-45, reflecting the HIV epidemic age distribution in endemic regions
keyStats.globalCases: Primarily endemic in Southeast Asia, southern China, northeastern India, and Hong Kong; travel-associated cases reported worldwide in returning visitors with HIV

definition: Penicillosis (ICD-10: B48.4) is an invasive fungal infection caused by Talaromyces marneffei, reclassified from the genus Penicillium on the basis of molecular phylogenetics in 2011. It is the only species in the genus Talaromyces known to cause systemic dimorphic mycosis in humans. At temperatures below 30°C in the environment, it grows as a mould producing characteristic red pigment that diffuses into culture media — a hallmark of the species. At 37°C inside the human body, it converts to a fission-yeast form that multiplies within macrophages and Kupffer cells of the liver, spleen, lymph nodes, bone marrow, and skin, spreading through the reticuloendothelial system. This intracellular lifestyle makes the infection both difficult to clear without intact cell-mediated immunity and difficult to diagnose without invasive sampling. The condition falls under infectious disease and HIV medicine in clinical practice. Outside Southeast Asia and southern China, the infection is rare and frequently misidentified on initial presentation because clinicians are unfamiliar with the pathogen.

typesClassification.description: By far the most common form, occurring in advanced HIV disease with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL; involves skin, lung, liver, spleen, bone marrow, and lymph nodes simultaneously, mimicking disseminated tuberculosis or histoplasmosis.

Localised respiratory form with cough, haemoptysis, and bilateral infiltrates on chest imaging; may present without the classic skin lesions and is easily confused with smear-negative tuberculosis in endemic areas.

Characteristic skin lesions are present in 60-80% of cases of disseminated disease; the umbilicated papules with central necrosis on the face, neck, and upper trunk are highly specific and act as a diagnostic clue.

Rare cases in transplant recipients, patients on corticosteroids, or those receiving biological agents; tends to present later in the disease course and is harder to recognise in the absence of HIV-related skin changes.

primarySymptoms: Prolonged fever lasting more than two weeks, often low-grade but occasionally spiking to 39-40°C, occurring in virtually all patients with disseminated disease.
Weight loss of more than 10% of body weight in many patients, reflecting the chronic systemic nature of the infection and the underlying HIV wasting.
Umbilicated papular skin lesions with central necrosis or crust on the face, neck, chest, and upper limbs, seen in 60-80% of cases and considered a clinical hallmark.
Anaemia, often moderate to severe, caused by bone marrow infiltration and resulting in pallor, fatigue, and exertional breathlessness.
Cough — dry or productive — with or without chest pain, from pulmonary infiltrates affecting the lungs.
Hepatomegaly and splenomegaly producing right-upper-quadrant discomfort and abdominal fullness as the reticuloendothelial organs are infiltrated.
Generalised lymphadenopathy, particularly of the cervical, axillary, and inguinal lymph nodes, from widespread reticuloendothelial involvement.
Diarrhoea and abdominal pain when the gastrointestinal tract is involved.
Fatigue and profound weakness disproportionate to the fever, related to bone marrow failure and anaemia.
Oral ulcers or palate lesions in some patients with extensive reticuloendothelial spread.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent fever with weight loss lasting more than two weeks in an HIV-positive patient in or from Southeast Asia.
New skin papules with central depression (umbilication) on the face in an immunocompromised individual.
Unexplained progressive anaemia in an HIV-positive patient with a CD4 count below 100 cells/µL.
Unexplained lymphadenopathy alongside fever and hepatomegaly in a patient from or returning from an endemic region.
emergencySigns: Respiratory failure with oxygen saturation below 90% from extensive bilateral pulmonary infiltrates.
Septic shock — high fever, hypotension, rapid heart rate, and altered consciousness — indicating overwhelming fungaemia.
Severe anaemia (haemoglobin below 7 g/dL) with haemodynamic instability requiring blood transfusion.
Rapidly progressive skin necrosis suggesting deep tissue spread and high fungal burden.
Pancytopenia with bone marrow failure causing both bleeding risk (low platelets) and severe infection susceptibility.

causes.cause: Inhalation of Talaromyces marneffei conidia from the environment
causes.description: The primary route of infection is inhalation of asexual spores (conidia) released by the mould form growing in soil, particularly in bamboo rat (Rhizomys species) burrows and soil disturbed during farming or construction; the fungus has been isolated from bamboo rat intestines and surrounding soil in endemic areas, establishing an animal-environment-human transmission chain.

causes.cause: Failure of cellular immunity to contain the inhaled fungus
causes.description: In immunocompetent hosts, alveolar macrophages phagocytose inhaled conidia and prevent dissemination; when CD4 T-cell counts fall below 100 cells/µL in HIV infection, macrophages cannot kill the intracellular yeast form and the organism escapes to seed the reticuloendothelial system and skin.

causes.cause: Reactivation of latent infection during immune deterioration
causes.description: Evidence from animal models and epidemiology suggests that initial exposure may establish a subclinical latent infection; immune deterioration from progressive HIV or immunosuppressive drugs may then allow reactivation and dissemination, analogous to latent tuberculosis reactivation.

causes.cause: Immunosuppressive therapy for transplantation or autoimmune disease
causes.description: Non-HIV patients on calcineurin inhibitors, high-dose corticosteroids, or biological agents that target TNF-α or other cytokines may develop penicillosis if they have lived in or travelled through endemic regions, as the same T-cell-dependent defect that allows HIV-related disease is reproduced pharmacologically.

causes.cause: Occupational and environmental soil exposure in endemic regions
causes.description: Agricultural workers, miners, and forestry workers in northern Thailand, Vietnam, Laos, southern China, and northeastern India face repeated high-dose environmental exposure; seasons of high rainfall that disturb soil increase conidia dispersal and correlate with peak incidence.

riskFactors.description: Over 90% of penicillosis cases occur at this level of immunosuppression; the risk rises sharply as CD4 counts fall, mirroring the pattern seen with other AIDS-defining opportunistic infections.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to Southeast Asia, southern China, or northeastern India
riskFactors.description: T. marneffei is geographically restricted; the vast majority of infections occur in endemic zones, and travel-associated cases are almost exclusively in people who visited these regions.

riskFactors.factor: Occupational soil exposure in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Farmers, construction workers, and those digging in soil rich in Rhizomys rat burrows face the highest environmental inoculum; seasonal peak incidence during the rainy season correlates with soil disturbance.

riskFactors.factor: Solid organ or haematopoietic stem cell transplantation
riskFactors.description: Recipients of organ transplants from or in endemic regions require awareness of penicillosis as a differential; anti-rejection immunosuppression replicates the cellular immunity defect of advanced HIV.

riskFactors.factor: Prolonged corticosteroid or biological immunosuppressant use
riskFactors.description: Agents that impair T-cell function, such as TNF inhibitors or high-dose prednisone, create risk in individuals with prior exposure history; the risk is lower than in HIV but well documented in case reports.
Protein-energy malnutrition in endemic regions impairs cellular immunity independently; historical cases of penicillosis in malnourished but HIV-negative patients are documented.

affectedDemographics: HIV-positive adults in Southeast Asia with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL
Agricultural and forestry workers in northern Thailand, Vietnam, Laos, and southern China
International travellers and immigrants from endemic regions who develop advanced HIV elsewhere
Solid organ transplant recipients with history of residence in or travel to endemic areas
Patients on biological agents (e.g. TNF inhibitors) with relevant geographic history
diagnosisOverview: Penicillosis is diagnosed by demonstrating Talaromyces marneffei in clinical specimens, most commonly bone marrow aspirate, blood, skin biopsy, or lymph node. The most rapid and accessible diagnostic method for clinicians outside mycology centres is skin-scraping smear or staining of tissue biopsy with Giemsa or Wright stain, which shows the pathognomonic intracellular yeast cells with a central septum — a feature distinguishing T. marneffei from Histoplasma capsulatum. Fungal cultures on blood agar or Sabouraud medium at 25°C produce mould colonies with a characteristic soluble red diffusible pigment within 5-7 days; the same culture at 37°C shows a creamy yeast phase. This thermal dimorphism with red pigment is definitively diagnostic. Blood cultures may be positive in 70-90% of disseminated cases when incubated specifically for fungi. Bone marrow biopsy has the highest single-specimen sensitivity (approximately 85%). Chest radiography or CT in pulmonary disease shows bilateral interstitial or alveolar infiltrates, sometimes with reticular nodular shadowing. Full blood count commonly shows anaemia, leucopenia, and thrombocytopenia from bone marrow infiltration. A serological latex agglutination test for T. marneffei antigen and antibody exists and is used in some endemic-region hospitals, providing results within hours. The differential diagnosis in an HIV-positive patient from Southeast Asia includes disseminated tuberculosis, cryptococcosis, histoplasmosis, disseminated Mycobacterium avium complex, and visceral leishmaniasis.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Rapidly identifies the intracellular yeast cells with central septum from umbilicated skin lesions; provides a diagnosis within hours without culture delay.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A clinician gently scrapes material from a skin lesion with a scalpel blade onto a glass slide; the procedure takes seconds and causes minimal discomfort.

diagnosticTests.test: Fungal blood culture with T. marneffei incubation protocol
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects fungaemia in disseminated disease with sensitivity of approximately 70-90% when fungal-specific culture bottles are used and incubated for up to 4 weeks.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn from a vein into fungal culture bottles and incubated; results may be available in 5-14 days, and the red pigment on culture confirms the species.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive single specimen, detecting T. marneffei in approximately 85% of disseminated cases by culture and histology, and identifying bone marrow infiltration as the cause of anaemia and pancytopenia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under local anaesthesia at the posterior iliac crest; a needle is inserted into the bone to withdraw marrow; mild to moderate pain and pressure are expected, lasting 5-10 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms T. marneffei by demonstrating mould-to-yeast conversion and the characteristic diffusible red pigment at 25°C — definitively distinguishing it from other fungi.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Laboratory procedure only; no additional patient sampling beyond the initial culture is needed.

diagnosticTests.test: Chest X-ray and CT of thorax and abdomen
diagnosticTests.purpose: Characterises pulmonary infiltrates and hepatosplenomegaly, helping to assess disease extent and guide biopsy planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard imaging; chest X-ray takes seconds and CT takes 10-20 minutes; abdominal CT may involve an intravenous contrast injection.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and liver function tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses bone marrow infiltration (anaemia, leucopenia, thrombocytopenia) and hepatic involvement; also monitors treatment safety.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available within a few hours.

diagnosticTests.test: T. marneffei antigen/antibody latex agglutination test
diagnosticTests.purpose: A rapid serological test used in endemic-region hospitals to support early diagnosis before culture results are available.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A serum sample is tested in the laboratory with results in 2-4 hours; availability is limited to endemic-region specialised laboratories.

treatmentOverview: Treatment follows a two-phase approach — induction to rapidly reduce fungal burden, followed by maintenance to prevent relapse — and is most effective when antiretroviral therapy is initiated concurrently to restore CD4-mediated immunity. The WHO and IDSA both recommend intravenous amphotericin B deoxycholate (0.6-1 mg/kg/day) or its lipid formulations for the induction phase in severe or disseminated penicillosis, continuing for two weeks. Liposomal amphotericin B (3-5 mg/kg/day) is preferred when the formulation is available because of significantly lower nephrotoxicity. Itraconazole (200 mg twice daily) is an acceptable alternative for less severe disease or where amphotericin is unavailable. Following induction, the maintenance phase uses oral itraconazole 200 mg daily for a minimum of 10 weeks, after which secondary prophylaxis at 200 mg daily is continued until CD4 count is sustained above 100 cells/µL on antiretroviral therapy. Voriconazole is a second-line alternative. Antiretroviral therapy should be started within 2-4 weeks of antifungal initiation in most cases; the timing balances immune reconstitution benefit against the risk of IRIS. Close monitoring of drug interactions between azoles and antiretroviral agents — which often share CYP450 pathways — is essential. With standard amphotericin B induction plus itraconazole maintenance, response rates exceed 80% and mortality falls from near 100% untreated to approximately 10-25%.

medicalTreatments.name: Liposomal amphotericin B (induction)
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous polyene antifungal given at 3-5 mg/kg/day for 14 days as the preferred induction therapy for severe or disseminated penicillosis; significantly less nephrotoxic than conventional formulation while maintaining fungicidal efficacy against T. marneffei.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response rates of approximately 85-90% after the 2-week induction course in patients who can receive the full dose.

medicalTreatments.name: Amphotericin B deoxycholate (induction)
medicalTreatments.description: Conventional intravenous amphotericin B at 0.6-1 mg/kg/day for 14 days; remains standard of care in resource-limited endemic regions where lipid formulations are unavailable; requires prehydration and close monitoring of renal function and electrolytes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate comparable to liposomal formulation in the induction phase, approximately 80-90%, but with significantly higher rates of nephrotoxicity and infusion reactions.

medicalTreatments.name: Itraconazole (induction and maintenance)
medicalTreatments.description: Oral triazole azole at 200 mg twice daily for induction in non-severe disease, then 200 mg once daily for 10-week consolidation, then secondary prophylaxis at 200 mg daily. Blood-level monitoring recommended given variable oral bioavailability.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Compared directly to amphotericin B in a 2018 randomised trial in Vietnam, itraconazole showed lower response rate (78% vs 92%) at 2 weeks but similar outcomes at 24 weeks in less severe disease.

medicalTreatments.name: Voriconazole
medicalTreatments.description: Second-generation triazole used as an alternative to itraconazole when the latter is not tolerated or available; case reports and small series document efficacy. Multiple drug interactions with antiretrovirals require careful management.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to itraconazole in published case series; no randomised trial data specific to penicillosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy (ART)
medicalTreatments.description: Not an antifungal but essential co-intervention; initiating or optimising ART to raise CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL is the most important determinant of preventing relapse. Typically started 2-4 weeks after antifungal initiation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: HIV patients on effective ART who achieve CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL can discontinue secondary prophylaxis; relapse rates fall from approximately 50% per year without ART to below 5% with immune reconstitution.

alternativeTreatments.name: Antiretroviral therapy initiation and optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Restoring immune function through ART is the definitive strategy for preventing recurrence; no herbal or alternative antifungal strategy has evidence in penicillosis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Nutritional support
alternativeTreatments.description: Repletion of calories and micronutrients in malnourished patients supports immune function and improves tolerance to antifungal drugs; important adjunct in resource-limited settings.

whySeeSpecialist: Penicillosis requires management by an infectious disease physician experienced with HIV-associated opportunistic infections and invasive fungal infections. Complex drug interactions between azole antifungals and antiretroviral therapy make co-management of both conditions technically demanding. Bone marrow biopsy, the most sensitive diagnostic test, requires a haematologist or specialist in invasive procedures. Patients in non-endemic countries need clinicians willing to consider the diagnosis in a traveller from Southeast Asia.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist at a centre that manages HIV opportunistic infections and has access to a mycology laboratory capable of performing thermal dimorphism cultures. In endemic regions, major hospitals in Bangkok, Ho Chi Minh City, and Hong Kong have established penicillosis management protocols. Outside endemic areas, HIV referral centres at academic medical institutions are most likely to have the expertise needed. Ask specifically whether the centre has managed penicillosis before.
preventionStrategies: Initiate and maintain effective antiretroviral therapy in all HIV-positive individuals to keep CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL, the threshold at which penicillosis risk becomes negligible.

HIV-positive patients from endemic regions with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL should discuss primary prophylaxis with itraconazole 200 mg daily with their clinician, particularly before returning to endemic areas.
Avoid unnecessary soil disturbance, farming in bamboo rat habitats, and high-risk environmental exposure in endemic areas during periods of low CD4 counts.
Discontinue secondary prophylaxis only after CD4 count has been sustained above 100 cells/µL for at least 6 months on effective ART, per WHO guidelines.
Non-HIV immunocompromised patients planning travel to or residence in endemic regions should discuss prophylactic antifungal strategies with their managing physician.
lifestyleModifications: Adhere strictly to antiretroviral therapy to maintain CD4 counts and prevent immunosuppression from slipping to levels that allow penicillosis to emerge or relapse.
Take itraconazole maintenance therapy consistently with a full meal to maximise absorption, and use the oral solution form if absorption is a concern.
Attend regular CD4 monitoring appointments to time safe discontinuation of antifungal prophylaxis.
Report skin lesions, new fever, cough, or unexplained weight loss immediately; early treatment dramatically improves survival.
Avoid raw soil contact and bamboo areas in endemic regions during periods of known low CD4 counts.

recommended: High-protein, calorie-adequate diet to correct malnutrition common in advanced HIV and to support immune recovery
Take itraconazole with food or cola to improve gastric acid-dependent absorption
Zinc and selenium-rich foods to support innate immunity during antifungal therapy
Adequate hydration, particularly during amphotericin B treatment to protect kidney function
avoid: Alcohol, which impairs liver function and reduces tolerance to azole antifungal hepatotoxicity monitoring
Grapefruit juice, which inhibits CYP3A4 and can raise itraconazole or voriconazole blood levels unpredictably
Antacids, H2 blockers, and proton pump inhibitors taken at the same time as itraconazole capsules — they reduce absorption significantly

exerciseGuidelines: During acute illness, bed rest is appropriate given the severity of disseminated penicillosis, the degree of anaemia, and the risk of falls from low platelet counts. As antifungal therapy takes effect and blood counts improve, graded mobilisation and then light aerobic activity can be reintroduced. Patients on ART who achieve immune reconstitution can return to normal physical activity; this also supports mental health recovery after a serious illness.
dailyManagement: Never miss doses of antifungal medication or antiretroviral therapy; sub-therapeutic drug levels allow relapse.
Monitor for itraconazole side effects — peripheral oedema, heart failure symptoms, and liver enzyme elevation; report these promptly.
Keep a symptom diary noting fever, skin changes, weight, and respiratory symptoms to identify early signs of relapse.
Attend scheduled CD4 count and liver and kidney function monitoring blood tests.
Seek emergency care immediately if fever returns with skin lesions or respiratory distress, which may signal treatment failure or relapse.
prognosis: With early diagnosis and standard antifungal treatment — induction amphotericin B followed by itraconazole maintenance — the overall mortality of penicillosis has fallen to approximately 10-25% in modern treated series, compared to near 100% in untreated advanced HIV. The strongest predictors of poor outcome are a high fungal burden at presentation, severe anaemia (haemoglobin below 8 g/dL), very low CD4 count (below 50 cells/µL), and delayed diagnosis. Patients who achieve sustained CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL on antiretroviral therapy have very low relapse rates and can safely discontinue secondary prophylaxis, effectively achieving long-term remission. Delay in diagnosis remains the most actionable target for improving outcomes, particularly outside endemic regions where clinicians may not consider the diagnosis in travellers.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and skin lesion improvement typically begin within 7-14 days of starting effective induction antifungal therapy. Anaemia corrects more slowly as bone marrow recovers, often over 4-8 weeks. The 2-week induction phase is followed by 10 weeks of consolidation itraconazole, after which secondary prophylaxis continues until CD4 count is sustainably above 100 cells/µL. Full functional recovery and weight restoration may take 3-6 months. Patients should not return to physically demanding work or travel to endemic areas until treatment is complete and CD4 counts confirm adequate immune recovery.
complications: Septic shock from overwhelming fungaemia — the most immediately life-threatening complication, presenting with hypotension, rapid heart rate, and altered mental status.
Bone marrow failure with severe anaemia, thrombocytopenia, and neutropenia requiring blood and platelet transfusions during the acute phase.
Hepatic dysfunction — both from direct liver infiltration and from antifungal drug toxicity, particularly with itraconazole and amphotericin B.

Immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) — paradoxical worsening of skin lesions, lymphadenopathy, or fever occurring 2-12 weeks after ART initiation, caused by recovering immune responses targeting the fungus.
Relapse — occurs in approximately 30-50% of patients without secondary prophylaxis or effective antiretroviral therapy; the risk is dramatically reduced by maintaining CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL.
Nephrotoxicity from conventional amphotericin B — acute kidney injury occurs in 20-30% of patients on the deoxycholate formulation without adequate prehydration.

supportResources.name: WHO — Guidelines for the Management of HIV-Associated Penicilliosis/Talaromycosis in Adults
supportResources.description: WHO guidance on fungal priority pathogens including Talaromyces marneffei, with treatment recommendations for endemic regions.

supportResources.name: NIH AIDSinfo — Opportunistic Infections Guidelines
supportResources.description: Comprehensive U.S. HIV opportunistic infection guidelines with a dedicated section on Talaromyces marneffei infection.

supportResources.name: CDC — Fungal Diseases in HIV/AIDS
supportResources.description: Overview of fungal infections affecting people with HIV, including rare endemic mycoses.

sources.title: Le T et al. Talaromyces marneffei Infection and Recent Advances in the Treatment of Systemic Fungal Infections. Future Microbiology, 2019
Le T et al. A Randomized Trial of Itraconazole or Amphotericin B for HIV-Associated Talaromycosis. New England Journal of Medicine, 2017
Vanittanakom N et al. Penicillium marneffei Infection and Recent Advances in Detection and Treatment. Clinical Microbiology Reviews, 2006
Supparatpinyo K et al. Disseminated Penicillium marneffei Infection in Southeast Asia. Lancet, 1994

confusedWithConditions.name: Pulmonary and disseminated tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause prolonged fever, weight loss, and pulmonary infiltrates in HIV patients; TB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis, confirmed by AFB smear and culture or GeneXpert, and treated with four-drug antibacterial therapy, not antifungals.

confusedWithConditions.name: Disseminated cryptococcosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cryptococcosis in HIV typically presents with meningitis and headache, is confirmed by India ink preparation and cryptococcal antigen testing, and lacks the umbilicated skin papules and diffusible red pigment on culture that characterise penicillosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Disseminated histoplasmosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Histoplasma capsulatum causes clinically identical disseminated disease — fever, skin lesions, hepatosplenomegaly — but is geographically centred in the Americas rather than Southeast Asia, and differs morphologically on culture and histopathology.

confusedWithConditions.name: Malaria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Malaria causes cyclic fever, anaemia, and splenomegaly in the same geographic regions as penicillosis, but is diagnosed by blood film or rapid antigen test and treated with antimalarials rather than antifungals.

relatedConditions.name: Disseminated cryptococcosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are AIDS-defining opportunistic fungal infections common in Southeast Asia, often co-managed by the same infectious disease and HIV medicine teams.

relatedConditions.name: Acute pulmonary histoplasmosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Histoplasmosis is the closest clinical and biological analogue to penicillosis — both are dimorphic fungal infections of the reticuloendothelial system in immunocompromised hosts, treated similarly with amphotericin B and itraconazole.

relatedConditions.name: Invasive pulmonary aspergillosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Aspergillosis and penicillosis both occur in severely immunocompromised patients with pulmonary infiltrates and are differentiated by organism morphology, culture characteristics, and galactomannan testing.

relatedConditions.name: Disseminated blastomycosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are disseminated dimorphic fungal infections with skin, lung, and multi-organ involvement in immunocompromised hosts, treated with the same antifungal classes.

faqs.question: What is penicillosis?
faqs.answer: Penicillosis is a serious fungal infection caused by Talaromyces marneffei (formerly Penicillium marneffei). It mainly affects people with HIV/AIDS who have very low CD4 counts, causing fever, weight loss, skin lesions, and anaemia. It is the third most common AIDS-defining infection in Southeast Asia.

faqs.question: Where is penicillosis most common?
faqs.answer: Penicillosis is endemic in Southeast Asia — particularly northern Thailand, Vietnam, and southern China — and northeastern India. Infection occurs mainly in people with HIV who live in or travel to these areas. Cases outside endemic regions almost always involve travellers or immigrants.

faqs.question: How do you get penicillosis?
faqs.answer: You inhale spores of Talaromyces marneffei from soil, particularly in areas disturbed by farming or near bamboo rat burrows. The fungus does not spread between people. Most healthy individuals clear the spores without developing disease; infection occurs when immune defences fail.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of penicillosis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are prolonged fever, weight loss, anaemia, and distinctive skin papules with a small central depression or crust on the face, neck, and chest. Hepatosplenomegaly and lymphadenopathy are also common. Pulmonary involvement causes cough and breathlessness.

faqs.question: How is penicillosis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is confirmed by culturing Talaromyces marneffei from blood, bone marrow, skin biopsy, or lymph node. The culture shows characteristic red diffusible pigment and converts between mould and yeast phases with temperature change. Skin scraping with Giemsa stain can give a rapid diagnosis within hours.

faqs.question: How is penicillosis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment uses intravenous amphotericin B for two weeks as induction therapy, followed by oral itraconazole for at least 10 weeks. Antiretroviral therapy is started concurrently to restore CD4 immunity. Secondary prophylaxis with itraconazole continues until CD4 count exceeds 100 cells/µL sustainably.

faqs.question: Can penicillosis be cured?
faqs.answer: Yes, with early treatment. Standard antifungal therapy achieves clinical response in over 80% of patients. Patients who start antiretroviral therapy and maintain CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL have very low relapse rates and can eventually stop antifungal prophylaxis. Delayed diagnosis significantly worsens outcomes.

faqs.question: What is the mortality rate of penicillosis?
faqs.answer: Untreated disseminated penicillosis in advanced HIV is nearly always fatal. With standard amphotericin B induction and itraconazole maintenance, mortality falls to approximately 10-25% in treated series. Poor prognostic factors include severe anaemia, very low CD4 count below 50 cells/µL, and delayed diagnosis.

faqs.question: Is penicillosis the same as a Penicillium mould allergy?
faqs.answer: No. Penicillosis is an invasive infection caused specifically by Talaromyces marneffei, previously called Penicillium marneffei. It has nothing to do with penicillin antibiotic allergy, common household Penicillium moulds that spoil food, or allergic reactions to environmental fungi.

faqs.question: Can I get penicillosis if I'm not HIV-positive?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is rare. Solid organ transplant recipients, patients on long-term immunosuppressants or biological agents targeting the immune system, and severely malnourished individuals can develop penicillosis if exposed in endemic areas. Healthy immunocompetent people almost never develop invasive infection.

faqs.question: What do penicillosis skin lesions look like?
faqs.answer: The characteristic skin lesions are small papules — raised bumps — with a central depression or necrotic crust (umbilicated papules), appearing most often on the face, neck, upper chest, and arms. They resemble molluscum contagiosum but occur in a context of severe systemic illness. They are present in 60-80% of disseminated cases.

faqs.question: Does penicillosis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Relapse occurs in approximately 30-50% of patients who do not receive secondary prophylaxis or effective antiretroviral therapy. Continuing itraconazole 200 mg daily until CD4 count is sustained above 100 cells/µL reduces this risk dramatically. Patients on effective ART who achieve immune recovery have relapse rates below 5%.

faqs.question: How long does treatment for penicillosis last?
faqs.answer: Induction with intravenous amphotericin B lasts 2 weeks, followed by 10 weeks of oral itraconazole consolidation. After that, daily itraconazole secondary prophylaxis continues until CD4 count has been above 100 cells/µL for at least 6 months on antiretroviral therapy. Total treatment may span 6-18 months or longer.

faqs.question: What blood tests are abnormal in penicillosis?
faqs.answer: Common findings include normocytic or microcytic anaemia, low white blood cell and platelet counts from bone marrow infiltration, elevated liver enzymes and bilirubin from hepatic involvement, and a very low CD4 count. Blood cultures are positive for T. marneffei in 70-90% of disseminated cases.

faqs.question: Is there a vaccine for penicillosis?
faqs.answer: No vaccine against Talaromyces marneffei currently exists. The most effective prevention is maintaining CD4 counts above 100 cells/µL through antiretroviral therapy. HIV-positive patients in endemic areas with CD4 below 100 cells/µL can discuss itraconazole primary prophylaxis with their clinician.

faqs.question: Can penicillosis affect the brain?
faqs.answer: CNS involvement is rare but has been reported in cases of disseminated penicillosis with meningitis or brain abscesses. More commonly, the central nervous system is spared and the illness centres on the reticuloendothelial system — bone marrow, liver, spleen, lymph nodes, and skin.

faqs.question: What is the difference between penicillosis and aspergillosis?
faqs.answer: Both are fungal infections but caused by entirely different organisms. Penicillosis is caused by Talaromyces marneffei, is geographically restricted to Southeast Asia, affects HIV patients with low CD4 counts, and causes skin lesions and reticuloendothelial spread. Aspergillosis is caused by Aspergillus species, is global in distribution, and primarily causes pulmonary infection in neutropenic patients.

faqs.question: Should travellers to Southeast Asia worry about penicillosis?
faqs.answer: Healthy immunocompetent travellers have essentially no risk. HIV-positive travellers to endemic areas with CD4 counts below 100 cells/µL should discuss prophylaxis and the diagnostic importance of skin lesions or prolonged fever with their HIV physician before travel. Travellers who develop fever and skin lesions after returning should mention their travel history.

faqs.question: What is immune reconstitution syndrome (IRIS) in penicillosis?
faqs.answer: IRIS occurs when antiretroviral therapy restores immune function and the recovering immune system mounts a strong inflammatory response against T. marneffei. This can cause paradoxical worsening of lymphadenopathy, skin lesions, or fever 2-12 weeks after starting ART. It is managed with anti-inflammatory treatment and does not indicate antifungal failure.

faqs.question: Is penicillosis the same as histoplasmosis?
faqs.answer: No. Both are dimorphic fungal infections of the reticuloendothelial system in immunocompromised patients, and they are clinically very similar. However, histoplasmosis is caused by Histoplasma capsulatum and is endemic in the Americas, while penicillosis is caused by Talaromyces marneffei and is endemic in Southeast Asia. They require the same antifungal drugs.

metaDescription: Penicillosis is an AIDS-defining fungal infection in Southeast Asia causing fever, skin lesions and anaemia. Learn diagnosis and antifungal treatment.
